title: No One is Too Small to Make a Difference

chapters.title: You know her name, but who is Greta Thunberg?
chapters.text:
There are few teenagers quite as famous as Greta Thunberg, but who is this highly passionate 16-year-old we all know by name?

Born in Sweden in 2003, Thunberg is world-famous for her activism on climate change. All of this began when she went on a school strike one Friday in August 2018. Rather than going to her usual lessons, Thunberg went to the Swedish Parliament building, sat down outside, and handed out fliers to passersby, highlighting the problems we’re facing in regards to global warming and worldwide climate change in general. This Friday strike now covers a huge 1 million children across the globe, named “Fridays For Future”.

Greta Thunberg started the “Fridays For Future” movement by refusing to go to school on a Friday and sitting in front of the Swedish Parliament building, highlighting the risks of climate change instead.

Of course, Greta’s parents were not very impressed that their daughter was skipping school on a Friday, but understanding her passion and her desire to make a difference, they supported her nonetheless. In essence, Greta is a passionate, strong and willful young woman, with a voice which you simply cannot ignore.

Now, just 16 years old, she has spoken at the UN COP24 in Katowice, addressed the Secretary General directly, and she was even nominated for a Nobel Peace Prize. At every rally she can get to, Thunberg is there. Despite that, she doesn’t fly and she does her best to live a low carbon life, therefore backing up her cries for action with her own.

Did you know? Greta Thunberg is sometimes called the “Joan of Arc of Climate Change”.

chapters.title: Three years maximum, that is all we have to make major strides towards change
chapters.text:
We have a maximum of three years, perhaps even less, in order to start reversing the climate damage we have done so far as a human race. Once those three years are up? A chain reaction of events may start to occur, possibly leading to the end of human civilization as we know it several years from now. We are thought to have around 12 years before all damage is completely irreversible, but nobody has a definite time frame on this, and it could be far less than we realize.

Let that sink in.

We have three years to cut carbon emissions drastically. If we don’t do that, in the near future, it really could be too late to turn back the clock.

At September 2018’s climate march in Stockholm, Thunberg talked about a statement from climate scientist Johan Rockstrom. Rockstrom’s research showed that we have three years at the very most to start working toward cutting down on emissions. This needs to be within the guidelines set out by the Paris Agreement, e.g. to keep global warming under 2 degrees celsius. This has now been revised and needs to be below 1.5 degrees celsius. Very little is being done to hit this target and most countries are way off the pace in achieving it.

The future of all the coming generations rests on your shoulders. ~ Greta Thunberg

It doesn’t all have to come from governments however, what you do as a person also counts. Every single person can do their bit to battle climate change, and if we do that, we can work together to keep global warming under that 1.5 degree celsius mark.

We need to keep global warming under 1.5 degrees celsius in order to ensure no irreversible damage is done to the planet.

Every single action, be it cutting down on your heating, recycling rather than throwing away, or switching to solar power, these all count, just as every single emission that makes itself up into the atmosphere counts.

Did you know? Greta’s speeches are all checked by scientists to ensure clarity and truth.

chapters.title: There are no grey areas when it comes to climate change
chapters.text:
Greta Thunberg has Asperger syndrome, but rather than see that as a negative point or allow it to hold her back, she embraces it for what it is and regards it as a talent. Through her Asperger’s, Greta sees everything as literally black and white. There are no middle sections where confusion reigns, no blurry waters and no grey areas. It is what it is, and that helps her to see and understand climate change as the hugely important and potentially catastrophic event it is.

To Thunberg, if the burning of fossil fuels is bad for the planet, she doesn’t understand why it is allowed to carry on. Surely it should have been banned the moment we realized how damaging it is? To her, if we want to save the planet, we just stop the emissions. It’s simple to Greta, but to the rest of the world, it seems to be a grey area that is deemed unimportant or slightly blurry.

There is no grey area when it comes to climate change. We either act now and make a difference, or carry on and face an extremely dangerous and uncertain future.

It’s not blurry, it’s serious and we need to wake up and smell the coffee right now.

Thunberg doesn’t understand that if people say climate change is a huge threat to civilization, why aren’t we doing more to save ourselves? Why are we carrying on like nothing is happening? Why are big businesses still drilling for oil, burning fossil fuels into the sky and pretending that there is no blame or consequence?

The whole subject really is black and white — we either do something now and change, or we stay as we are and perhaps cease to have a future in a few years from now.

Did you know? Asperger’s syndrome is on the autism spectrum and is a disorder which creates difficulties in social interactions and communication.

chapters.title: If we know what we need to do, why aren’t we doing it?
chapters.text:
Perhaps the most frustrating thing about climate change is the fact that nobody in power is listening! It’s down to school children like Thunberg and activists marching through the world’s major cities to try to create a buzz and action, but without the backing of those in power, all of this comes to very little.

Of course, small actions by every single person will always make a difference and you should continue doing whatever you can, but without major changes by Government and business figures, the major shift in behavior and effect won’t be quite enough.

If everyone makes changes to their lifestyles, a difference can be made, but businesses and government figures need to wake up and do more in order to make a real, lasting difference.

Greta highlights countries such as the UK and her home country of Sweden as needing to do more. These are countries which need to reduce their emissions by at least 15% to remain under the target, and this doesn’t include the locked in emissions which are hidden by air pollution, which could drastically contribute towards the problem. This means that even if we manage to completely stop burning fossil fuels, there is still a 1.1 degree celsius to contend with, which we can do nothing about.

In addition, there are around 200 species of wildlife becoming extinct every single day.

Every single day.

Nobody talks about this, nobody is screaming or crying about it. Why? Surely we should be devastated!

Around 200 species of wildlife are becoming extinct daily due to shifts in the ecosystems, all thanks to climate change.

If we know what the problem is, and if we know what we need to do about it, why is nobody doing it? Perhaps this is why climate change is the single most frustrating topic of our lifetimes.

chapters.title: Act now so your children have a future
chapters.text:
Perhaps the most famous public speech that Thunberg has made to date is at the United Nations Climate Change Conference in Katowice, Poland. That was in December 2018 and at this conference she made a rousing and very passionate speech about the future, highlighting the fact that nobody is too small to make a difference to climate change. If a group of school children can hit the headlines for not going to school on a Friday, can you imagine what a huge country such as the USA could do for climate change?

At this speech, Thunberg mentioned that in 2078 she will be 75 and she wonders if she will have children and grandchildren around her. If she does, she isn’t sure what she will tell them when they ask why people didn’t do more to change the world whilst they had the chance, before irreversible damage was done.

You say that you love your children above anything else. And yet, you are stealing their future. ~ Greta Thunberg

Thunberg’s rousing speech won plaudits and from there she has gone on to speak at other United Nations conferences and other rallies and Government events around the globe. When traveling to these, Greta travels on her emission-free boat and refuses to fly.

Did you know? In 2018, Greta Thunberg was included in TIME Magazine’s list of Most Influential Teens.

chapters.title: Would you act faster if your literal home was on fire?
chapters.text:
A term that Greta uses in many of her speeches is “our house is on fire”. When you think about it, it really is. We’re frying from the inside out, with increasing temperatures year upon year, forests burning around the world and warmer seas than ever before. You might think that a warmer summer is a good thing, but when you delve deeper into what is causing those temperature increases, it’s far from pleasant and far more sinister.

According to the IPCC, we are under 12 years away from completely being unable to undo all of our mistakes. That means we need to cut our CO2 emissions by half at the very least and that doesn’t take into account feedback loops or other issues which we don’t have any control over, such as the methane which is being released by the melting Arctic frosts.

According to the IPCC, we are 12 years away from completely irreversible damage and possible events which could lead to the end of civilization.

Speaking at a conference in Davos, Thunberg highlighted the fact that business success has come at a huge price tag. We have to own up to the fact that whatever we have done, we have failed and we’re slowly ruining the planet we call home. The media have failed to highlight the issue with the public, businesses have failed by continuing to burn fossil fuels, and governments have failed by simply ignoring the severity of what we are faced with.

Our house is burning, but if it was your literal house, your own home you have paid for with your own cash, you’d be far quicker to douse the flames, wouldn’t you?

The world is becoming hotter and forests are burning every year. Your house is on fire — why aren’t you doing anything about dousing the flames?

We still have time to turn the situation around but we need to act fast. If we don’t? We may not be around to see the most severe consequences for ourselves. Civilization as we know it could be over.

Did you know? Every single year, a coastal ecosystem which is larger in size than NYC is completely destroyed.

chapters.title: “Don’t you think that a 16-year-old can speak for herself?”
chapters.text:
Greta Thunberg, for all her influence and passion, often gets a lot of hate on social media and from worldwide figures. It’s unfair to talk this way about a school child, but in her usual strong-willed way, Greta addressed this on Facebook.

She explained her story, told everyone that she isn’t surprised that she has so much hate turned towards her, but that she takes it as proof that she is stirring up deep feelings, and feelings which may move others to act in a positive way for the environment.

She acknowledges that her parents weren’t keen on her missing school to strike on Fridays, but they supported her regardless. When she did this, she gave out fliers and posted what she was doing on social media. Unsurprisingly, it went viral. Many people took notice and one of those people was Ingmar Rentzhog, a Swedish entrepreneur with an interest in climate change.

Thunberg has never been paid for any of the work she does for climate activism and she doesn’t have a team of PR people behind her — she does it all herself and she isn’t paid for it. Contrary to popular opinion, she also writes her own speeches, although they are fact-checked to ensure credibility when speaking at large events.

Greta Thunberg is not paid for the activism work she does, it is all done for free and because of her passion for the cause.

Greta sees her Asperger’s as a gift, something which sets her apart from everyone else. She feels that if she didn’t have the diagnosis, she perhaps wouldn’t see things as she does, perhaps not quite as black and white. Then, perhaps she wouldn’t be as powerful or as passionate when speaking about what she feels. Many people say that it sounds like adults write her speeches, but to counteract that, she simply says “don’t you think that a 16-year-old can speak for herself?”

chapters.title: It’s time to panic, and then act
chapters.text:
We’re often told not to panic. We’re told that panicking isn’t useful because it doesn’t allow us to see things clearly or to make strong decisions. In this case however, Thunberg wants you to panic, she wants Governments, businesses, and individuals to panic. Only when we panic about something do we feel like we need to do something about it.

We live in a world where children are being forced to sacrifice their education to get people to stand up and take notice. Movie premieres, football games, and social media parties get more attention than the major issues in the world. Put simply, climate change is the biggest risk we have faced in terms of the risk to humanity.

Climate change is the biggest threat to humanity the world has ever faced, yet we’re riddled with inactivity on the subject.

At the Goldene Kamera Film And TV Awards in Berlin, March 2019, Greta spoke to an audience full of celebrities and she asked them to speak up on the issues that matter. People follow and listen to celebrities, and their voice can be the springboard towards action that could make a huge difference to our lives. By speaking up, celebrities can turn small groups of people into hugely influential movements.

Thankfully, more and more celebrities are doing just that, but it is enough? Governments need to take action, forcing businesses to make the right decisions. It’s not enough to blame the fragile economy, it’s not enough to say that we need these businesses to ensure we all keep our jobs and put food on the table — there are other ways to do that, but there is no other way to save the planet.

Celebrities are starting to speak out and inspire people to take action, but everyone can do that — be strong enough to stand up and say “we need to panic, we need to do more”.

In April 2019, Thunberg pushed this point forward once more, this time at the European Parliament in Strasbourg. Our entire civilization is threatened and we need to panic, we need to worry, take action and be serious about the entire situation. If your home was burning, you would panic, but nobody panics about the environment.

It’s time to panic.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The risk we face at the hands of climate change is something we have brought on ourselves over the years. For now, it is reversible, but we need to take serious action and do something about it without delay. The problem? Governments aren’t doing enough, some aren’t even taking it seriously, and the economic impact is being put before the existence of civilization.

Isn’t that ironic?

Whilst we need to take major action, this is a subject which can be impacted by every single person on the planet. You need to start changing the way you live. Use less energy, switch to solar power, recycle, cut down on your emissions and focus on living a life which aims to help the climate fight, rather than battling against it. If you want to, explore a vegan life, but whatever you do, make sure that you take action, rather than simply waiting for the irreversible effects to begin.

Try this

Think carefully about where the energy for your household comes from. Could you switch to a more climate-friendly choice?

How many journeys in the car do you take each week? Try cycling or walking at least half of the time, and work up to making that all of the time.

Rather than flying on your next vacation, could you travel in a climate-friendly way? Perhaps by emission-free boat?

Read it till the end?
